16. 16. 📌 Summary

  • Critical period: Organogenesis 3–8 weeks (5–10 weeks LMP) = highest structural teratogen risk
  • FDA Categories: A (safe), B (animal safe), C (animal risk), D (human risk, may use if benefit>risk), X (contraindicated)
  • Category X (avoid always): Isotretinoin, Thalidomide, Methotrexate, Ribavirin, Mycophenolate, Finasteride
  • Category D (major teratogens): Valproate (NTD, neuro), Carbamazepine/Phenytoin (NTD, syndromes), Warfarin (chondrodysplasia, ICH), ACEi/ARB (renal dysplasia), DOACs, Statins, Lithium (Ebstein's), Tetracyclines (teeth/bone), Sulfonamides (kernicterus 3rd tri), Fluoroquinolones (cartilage), Aminoglycosides (ototoxicity)
  • HTN: Methyldopa/Labetalol 1st line; Nifedipine 2nd; ACEi/ARB/Thiazides/Atenolol avoid
  • Anticoagulation: LMWH throughout for VTE/AF; Mechanical valve: LMWH 1st tri, Warfarin 2nd/3rd (if ≤5mg), LMWH 36w
  • Epilepsy: Lamotrigine/Levetiracetam preferred; Valproate avoid; Folic acid 5mg; TDM (↑ clearance); Vit K at birth if enzyme inducers
  • Diabetes: Insulin gold standard; Metformin B (GDM); Others avoid
  • Depression: Sertraline preferred; Paroxetine avoid (cardiac); Neonatal adaptation syndrome (self-limiting)
  • Antibiotics: Safe — Penicillins, Cephalosporins, Carbapenems, Azithromycin, Clindamycin, Vancomycin; Avoid — Tetracyclines, Fluoroquinolones, Aminoglycosides, Sulfonamides
  • Lactation: L1/L2 safe (paracetamol, ibuprofen, penicillins, sertraline, labetalol, LMWH); Avoid L4/L5: Lithium, Amiodarone, Diazepam, Codeine, Aspirin, Combined OCP
  • Pre-conception: Folic acid 400μg (5mg high risk); Switch teratogens; Immunity; TSH<2.5; HbA1c<48

17. 17. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Critical period for structural teratogenicity:

A. 0–2 weeks B. 3–8 weeks C. 9–12 weeks D. 2nd trimester

Answer: B. Organogenesis 3–8 weeks post-conception (5–10 weeks LMP).

  1. FDA Category X drug (contraindicated in pregnancy):

A. Warfarin B. Valproate C. Isotretinoin D. Lithium

Answer: C. Isotretinoin = Category X. Warfarin/Valproate/Lithium = Category D.

  1. First-line antihypertensive in pregnancy:

A. Ramipril B. Methyldopa C. Atenolol D. Hydrochlorothiazide

Answer: B. Methyldopa (most evidence) and Labetalol are 1st line. ACEi/ARB contraindicated.

  1. Anticoagulation for mechanical mitral valve in pregnancy:

A. Warfarin throughout B. DOAC throughout C. LMWH 1st tri, Warfarin 2nd/3rd (if dose≤5mg), LMWH 36w D. Aspirin only

Answer: C. Standard regimen. Or LMWH throughout.

  1. Preferred antiepileptic in pregnancy:

A. Valproate B. Carbamazepine C. Lamotrigine D. Phenytoin

Answer: C. Lamotrigine (and Levetiracetam) preferred. Valproate/CBZ/PHT = Category D teratogens.

  1. Folic acid dose for woman on antiepileptics planning pregnancy:

A. 400μg B. 5mg C. 1mg D. 10mg

Answer: B. 5mg daily for high-risk (AEDs, diabetes, BMI>30, previous NTD, family history, folate antagonists).

  1. SSRI with most safety data in pregnancy:

A. Paroxetine B. Fluoxetine C. Sertraline D. Citalopram

Answer: C. Sertraline preferred. Paroxetine = Category D (cardiac malformations).

  1. Antibiotic contraindicated in pregnancy (Category D):

A. Amoxicillin B. Cephalexin C. Doxycycline D. Azithromycin

Answer: C. Tetracyclines = Category D (tooth discoloration, bone growth inhibition).

  1. Drug to AVOID in breastfeeding (Hale L4/L5):

A. Paracetamol B. Ibuprofen C. Lithium D. Sertraline

Answer: C. Lithium = L4 (high milk transfer, infant toxicity). Paracetamol/Ibuprofen/Sertraline = L1/L2.

  1. Neonatal adaptation syndrome caused by:

A. Benzodiazepines B. SSRIs (3rd trimester) C. Opioids D. Beta-blockers

Answer: B. SSRIs in 3rd trimester → jitteriness, irritability, feeding difficulty, respiratory distress, hypoglycaemia. Self-limiting 2–14 days.

18. 18. 📋 SBAs (10)

  1. 32F with mechanical mitral valve (ON-X) on warfarin 4mg/day plans pregnancy. Best management?

A. Continue warfarin throughout B. Switch to therapeutic LMWH pre-conception C. Switch to apixaban D. Stop anticoagulation

Answer: B. Pre-conception switch to LMWH. Warfarin embryopathy risk 6–12% in 1st trimester.

  1. 26F on valproate 1000mg for juvenile myoclonic epilepsy discovers she's 6 weeks pregnant. Immediate action?

A. Stop valproate immediately B. Continue valproate; urgent neurology referral; start folic acid 5mg C. Switch to carbamazepine D. Reduce valproate by 50%

Answer: B. Do NOT stop abruptly (seizure risk). Urgent specialist review for cross-taper to LTG/LEV. Folic acid 5mg immediately.

  1. Pregnant woman at 28 weeks with UTI. E. coli sensitive to all. Best antibiotic?

A. Ciprofloxacin B. Cefalexin C. Nitrofurantoin D. TMP-SMX

Answer: B. Cephalosporins (cefalexin) = Category B, safe. Ciprofloxacin = avoid (cartilage). Nitrofurantoin = avoid near term (haemolysis). TMP-SMX = avoid 3rd tri (kernicterus).

  1. Breastfeeding mother needs analgesia for mastitis. Safe choice?

A. Codeine B. Aspirin C. Ibuprofen D. Diazepam

Answer: C. Ibuprofen = L1 (safe). Codeine = risk respiratory depression. Aspirin = Reye's. Diazepam = sedation.

  1. Woman on lithium 600mg for bipolar disorder becomes pregnant. Fetal risk?

A. Neural tube defects B. Ebstein's anomaly (tricuspid dysplasia) C. Limb reduction D. Renal dysplasia

Answer: B. Lithium = Ebstein's anomaly (0.1–0.2%). Valproate/CBZ/PHT = NTD. Warfarin = chondrodysplasia. ACEi = renal dysplasia.

  1. Folic acid 5mg indicated in all EXCEPT:

A. Epilepsy on carbamazepine B. BMI 32 C. Previous uncomplicated pregnancy D. Diabetes

Answer: C. High-risk indications: AEDs, diabetes, BMI>30, previous NTD, family history, folate antagonists. Previous uncomplicated pregnancy = standard 400μg.

  1. ACE inhibitor fetopathy features:

A. Chondrodysplasia punctata B. Renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia C. Ebstein's anomaly D. Neural tube defects

Answer: B. ACEi/ARB 2nd/3rd tri → fetal renal dysplasia → oligohydramnios → pulmonary hypoplasia, skull hypoplasia.

  1. Lamotrigine in pregnancy — dose adjustment?

A. No change B. Increase dose (↑ clearance 2–3x); TDM guided C. Decrease dose D. Stop

Answer: B. Lamotrigine clearance ↑ 2–3x in pregnancy (↑ UGT). TDM essential; dose often doubled.

  1. Combined oral contraceptive in breastfeeding — effect?

A. Increases milk supply B. Decreases milk supply (oestrogen) C. No effect D. Causes infant thrombosis

Answer: B. Oestrogen suppresses prolactin → ↓ milk supply. Use progesterone-only methods.

  1. Warfarin embryopathy — characteristic feature:

A. Chondrodysplasia punctata (stippled epiphyses) B. Ebstein's anomaly C. Neural tube defects D. Renal dysplasia

Answer: A. Warfarin embryopathy = chondrodysplasia punctata, nasal hypoplasia, CNS/eye defects. Ebstein's = lithium. NTD = valproate/CBZ/PHT. Renal dysplasia = ACEi.

topics.title: Prescribing In Special Populations
topics.html:
[!info]
Heading Hub for Davidson Chapter 2: Clinical therapeutics and good prescribing.
Davidson alignment: Section on Prescribing in Special Populations (SRJ Maxwell).

1. 1. Scope

Pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic alterations in renal impairment, hepatic impairment, elderly, pregnancy/lactation, and paediatrics; dose adjustment principles and contraindicated drugs.

3. 3. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

1. Renal Dosing Quick Reference (eGFR-based)

eGFR (mL/min)Dosing Adjustment Principle
≥60Normal dose
30-59Reduce dose OR extend interval (check specific drug)
15-29Significant dose reduction; extend interval; avoid nephrotoxins
<15 (Dialysis)Post-HD dosing for renally cleared drugs; avoid drugs dialysed out

Cockcroft-Gault (CrCl) for Drug Dosing:

CrCl (mL/min) = (140 - Age) × Weight (kg) × (0.85 if female) / (72 × SCr mg/dL)
Use actual body weight unless obese (then adjusted body weight)

2. Common Drug Renal Adjustments (CKD Stages G3-G5)

DrugeGFR 30-59eGFR 15-29eGFR <15 / Dialysis
MetforminMax 1g BD (caution)Contraindicated (lactic acidosis)Contraindicated
ACEi/ARBStandard dose; monitor K, CrReduce dose; monitor closelyStart low; monitor; hold if K>5.5
DOACsRivaroxaban 15mg OD (AF) / 10mg OD (VTE)Apixaban 2.5mg BD / Dabigatran 110mg BD / Avoid RivaroxabanAvoid DOACs; Use Warfarin
AminoglycosidesExtended interval; monitor levelsExtended interval; monitor levelsPost-HD dosing
VancomycinStandard dose; AUC-guidedStandard dose; AUC-guidedPost-HD dosing (15-20mg/kg)
Gabapentin/PregabalinReduce doseSignificant reductionDose post-HD
AllopurinolReduce dose (100mg/day max if <30)100mg/day max100mg post-HD
ColchicineReduce dose (0.5mg OD)0.5mg alternate daysAvoid / 0.5mg post-HD

3. Hepatic Impairment (Child-Pugh)

ClassScoreDosing Approach
A (Mild)5-6Minor reduction for high-extraction drugs; standard for most
B (Moderate)7-9Reduce dose 50% for high-extraction; 25% for low-extraction; avoid hepatotoxins
C (Severe)10-15Avoid most hepatically cleared drugs; use alternatives

High-Extraction Drugs (Flow-limited): Propranolol, Lidocaine, Morphine, Verapamil, Nitrates

Low-Extraction Drugs (Capacity-limited): Warfarin, Phenytoin, Diazepam, Theophylline

4. Pregnancy Categories (FDA → Phased Out; Use Narrative Labelling)

Old CategoryMeaningExamples
AControlled studies show no riskLevothyroxine, Folic acid, Pyridoxine
BAnimal studies no risk; no human studies OR animal risk but human studies no riskPenicillins, Cephalosporins, Metformin, Insulin, Paracetamol
CAnimal risk; no human studies OR no animal/human dataMost antidepressants (SSRIs), Antihypertensives (except ACEi/ARB), Corticosteroids
DPositive human risk; benefits may outweighLithium, Valproate, Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Warfarin, ACEi/ARB, DOACs, Statins
XContraindicated; risk > benefitIsotretinoin, Thalidomide, Methotrexate, Ribavirin, Mycophenolate, Misoprostol

UK/EU System: No letter categories; use Summary of Product Characteristics (SmPC) Section 4.6 — "Contraindicated", "Not recommended", "Use only if clearly needed", "May be used"

5. Teratogenic Critical Periods

Organ SystemCritical Period (Post-conception)
Neural tubeDays 18-28 (Week 3-4)
HeartDays 20-50 (Week 3-7)
LimbsDays 24-46 (Week 4-7)
EyesDays 21-42 (Week 3-6)
PalateDays 42-56 (Week 6-8)
External genitaliaWeeks 7-12
Brain (functional)Throughout pregnancy (especially 2nd/3rd trimester)

6. Key Teratogens (Avoid in Pregnancy)

  • Valproate — Neural tube defects (1-2%), developmental delay, autism (↑ risk vs other AEDs)
  • Carbamazepine — Neural tube defects, craniofacial abnormalities
  • Lithium — Ebstein's anomaly (cardiac), 1st trimester
  • Warfarin — Warfarin embryopathy (chondrodysplasia punctata, nasal hypoplasia) 1st trimester; CNS bleeding 2nd/3rd
  • ACEi/ARB — Fetal renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia, skull ossification defects (2nd/3rd trimester)
  • Isotretinoin — Severe CNS, cardiac, thymic, craniofacial defects
  • Methotrexate — Fetal death, craniofacial, limb defects
  • Statins — Animal teratogenicity; theoretical risk to cholesterol synthesis

7. Safe in Pregnancy (Common)

IndicationPreferred Drug(s)
HypertensionLabetalol, Methyldopa, Nifedipine (MR)
AnticoagulationLMWH (Enoxaparin) — All trimesters; Warfarin 2nd/3rd only if mechanical valve
DiabetesInsulin; Metformin (2nd line, growing evidence)
EpilepsyLamotrigine, Levetiracetam (Lowest teratogenicity); Folic acid 5mg daily
DepressionSertraline, Fluoxetine, Citalopram (SSRIs preferred)
AsthmaContinue all inhalers (ICS, LABA, SABA); Montelukast
PainParacetamol (1st line); Avoid NSAIDs 3rd trimester
NauseaDoxylamine + Pyridoxine (1st line); Metoclopramide, Ondansetron (2nd line)

8. Paediatric Dosing

MethodFormulaUse Case
Weight-basedDose (mg/kg) × Weight (kg)Most drugs
BSA-basedDose (mg/m²) × BSA (m²)Chemotherapy, some antibiotics
Age-based(Age + 4) / 24 × Adult dose (Young's Rule)Approximation only
BSA (Mosteller)√[Height(cm) × Weight(kg) / 3600]Most accurate for chemo

5. 5. Tags

#medicine #clinical-therapeutics #davidson #hub #special-populations #renal-dosing #hepatic-dosing #pregnancy-prescribing #paediatric-dosing #fcps #mrcp

6. 6. 📌 Summary

Prescribing in special populations is a core FCPS/MRCP prescribing topic. Master renal dosing (eGFR/Cockcroft-Gault), hepatic dosing (Child-Pugh, high vs low extraction), pregnancy/lactation safety (teratogenicity, FDA/UK categories), paediatric dosing (weight/BSA/age), and elderly prescribing (frailty, Beers/STOPP, deprescribing). Accurate dose adjustment prevents toxicity and therapeutic failure in vulnerable patients.

7. 7. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Renal dosing — metformin contraindication threshold?

A. eGFR <60

B. eGFR <45

C. eGFR <30 (contraindicated); eGFR 30-45: max 1g BD

D. eGFR <15 only

  1. DOAC dosing in CKD — rivaroxaban for AF at eGFR 30-49?

A. 20mg OD

B. 15mg OD

C. 10mg OD

D. Contraindicated

  1. Child-Pugh Class C — dosing principle?

A. Standard doses for all drugs

B. Avoid most hepatically cleared drugs; avoid hepatotoxins; use alternatives

C. Reduce all drugs by 25%

D. No dose adjustment needed

  1. High-extraction drugs (flow-limited) — which is correct?

A. Warfarin, Phenytoin

B. Propranolol, Lidocaine, Morphine, Verapamil, Nitrates

C. Phenytoin, Theophylline, Diazepam

D. Digoxin, Atenolol, Gabapentin

  1. Pregnancy — ACE inhibitor risk in 2nd/3rd trimester?

A. Cardiac malformations

B. Fetal renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia, skull defects

C. Neural tube defects

C. Limb reduction defects

  1. Teratogen — valproate associated with:

A. Cardiac defects only

B. Neural tube defects (1-2%), developmental delay, autism

C. Cardiac defects + cleft palate only

D. Limb reduction defects only

  1. ACE inhibitor in pregnancy — when absolutely contraindicated?

A. 1st trimester only

B. 2nd and 3rd trimester (fetal renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia)

C. All trimesters equally

D. Only if woman has hypertension

  1. Paediatric dosing — most accurate method for chemotherapy?

A. Weight-based (mg/kg)

B. BSA-based (mg/m²) with Mosteller formula

C. Age-based (Young's Rule)

D. Flat adult dose

  1. Elderly — STOPP criterion for statin in primary prevention?

A. >65 years

B. >85 years

C. >75 years

D. >70 years

  1. Paediatric dosing — Young's Rule formula?

A. (Age × Weight) / Adult Dose

B. (Age + 4) / 24 × Adult Dose

C. Weight (kg) × 2 / Adult Dose

D. BSA (m²) × Adult Dose

  1. Drug dosing in haemodialysis — when to give renally cleared drug?

A. Pre-dialysis

B. Post-dialysis

C. Mid-dialysis

D. Any time

8. 8. 📋 SBAs (5)

  1. 85F, eGFR 28, T2DM on metformin 1g BD. Management?

A. Continue 1g BD

B. Reduce to 500mg BD (max 1g/day if eGFR 30-45); contraindicated if <30

C. Switch to gliclazide

C. Stop metformin

Answer: B

  1. 32F, 12 weeks pregnant, epilepsy on valproate 1g BD. Folic acid 5mg prescribed. Best AED switch?

A. Carbamazepine

B. Lamotrigine or Levetiracetam (lowest teratogenicity); Folic acid 5mg daily

C. Phenytoin

D. Topiramate

Answer: B

  1. 70M, Child-Pugh B cirrhosis, needs analgesia for fracture. Safe opioid?

A. Morphine (standard dose)

B. Fentanyl/Alfentanil (low hepatic extraction, safe in liver disease); avoid morphine/codeine

C. Codeine 60mg

C. Tramadol 100mg

Answer: B

  1. 5-year-old, 18kg, needs amoxicillin for pneumonia. Dose?

A. 125mg TDS

B. 25mg/kg/dose = 450mg TDS (50mg/kg/day in divided doses)

C. 500mg TDS

D. 250mg BD

Answer: B

  1. 85F, frail, on 12 medications including lorazepam, digoxin, furosemide. STOPP criterion?

A. Statin primary prevention >85y

B. Benzodiazepine >4w (falls, cognitive impairment) — STOPP B2

C. PPI >8w

D. Warfarin >1y VTE

Answer: B

9. 9. 🔑 Answer Keys

MCQsSBAs
1-C, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B

10. 10. 🎤 Viva Questions (Expected Answers)

#QuestionExpected Answer
1Metformin in renal impairment — dosing thresholds?eGFR ≥45: full dose; 30-44: max 1g BD (500mg BD); 15-29: contraindicated (lactic acidosis risk); <15/dialysis: contraindicated.
2Child-Pugh classification — classes and dosing?A (5-6): mild, minor reduction for high-extraction. B (7-9): moderate, reduce high-extraction 50%, low-extraction 25%, avoid hepatotoxins. C (10-15): severe, avoid most hepatically cleared drugs.
3High vs low extraction drugs — examples and hepatic dosing impact?High-extraction (flow-limited): Propranolol, Lidocaine, Morphine, Verapamil, Nitrates — dose depends on hepatic blood flow; reduce in liver disease. Low-extraction (capacity-limited): Warfarin, Phenytoin, Diazepam, Theophylline — dose depends on intrinsic clearance/enzyme activity.
4Teratogenic critical periods — neural tube, heart, limbs?Neural tube: Days 18-28 (Week 3-4). Heart: Days 20-50 (Week 3-7). Limbs: Days 24-46 (Week 4-7).
5. Key teratogens — valproate, warfarin, ACEi, lithium?Valproate: NTD 1-2%, developmental delay, autism. Warfarin: Embryopathy (chondrodysplasia, nasal hypoplasia) 1st tri; CNS bleed 2nd/3rd. ACEi: Fetal renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia (2nd/3rd tri). Lithium: Ebstein's anomaly (cardiac) 1st tri. ACEi/ARB Contraindicated 2nd/3rd tri.
6. Warfarin in pregnancy — when can it be used?Mechanical heart valve only (with specialist oversight); otherwise contraindicated (teratogenic 1st tri, CNS bleeding 2nd/3rd). LMWH preferred for VTE/AF in pregnancy.
7. Paediatric dosing — methods and when to use each?Weight-based (mg/kg) = most drugs; BSA-based (mg/m²) = chemotherapy, some antibiotics (Mosteller formula); Age-based (Young's Rule) = approximation only.
7STOPP/START in elderly — key criteria?STOPP: Benzos >4w, PPI >8w, NSAID in HF/CKD/ulcer, Warfarin >1y for VTE, Aspirin 1° prev >70, Statin 1° prev >85, Anticholinergic burden. START: Vit D+Ca in OP, ACEi/ARB in HFrEF, BB in HFrEF, Statin in CVD, Anticoag in AF, Bisphosphonate in OP.
8DOAC in CKD — rivaroxaban for AF dosing by eGFR?≥50: 20mg OD; 15-49: 15mg OD; <15: Avoid (use warfarin/LMWH). CrCl 15-49: 15mg OD.
9Pregnancy — ACE inhibitor risk in 2nd/3rd trimester?Fetal renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios, pulmonary hypoplasia, skull defects. Contraindicated 2nd/3rd trimester.
10. Paediatric dosing — Mosteller formula?BSA (m²) = √[Height(cm) × Weight(kg) / 3600]. Most accurate for chemo dosing.

11. 11. 🧩 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
eGFR = CrCl for dosingNO. eGFR (MDRD/CKD-EPI) ≠ CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault). Use CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault) for drug dosing — it estimates creatinine clearance better for dosing.
All drugs need renal dose adjustmentNO. Only renally cleared drugs (eGFR <60). Check each drug individually.
Child-Pugh C = no drugsNO. Some drugs still usable with careful titration and monitoring; many require avoidance.
All statins safe in liver diseaseNO. Statins metabolised by CYP3A4 (simvastatin, atorvastatin, lovastatin) need caution in liver disease. Pravastatin, rosuvastatin, fluvastatin, pitavastatin preferred.
All antibiotics safe in pregnancyNO. Tetracyclines, Aminoglycosides, Fluoroquinolones, Sulfonamides, Metronidazole (1st tri) = contraindicated/risk. Penicillins, Cephalosporins, Macrolides, Clindamycin = generally safe.
All antiepileptics teratogenicNO. Lamotrigine, Levetiracetam = lowest risk. Valproate highest risk. Carbamazepine/Phentoin intermediate.
All DOACs contraindicated in CKDNO. Apixaban 2.5mg BD (eGFR 15-29), Dabigatran 110mg BD (CrCl 30-49), Rivaroxaban 15mg OD (CrCl 15-49). Warfarin for eGFR <15/dialysis.
Elderly = just reduce all dosesNO. Individualised deprescribing (STOPP/START). Some drugs need full dose (e.g., antibiotic for severe infection).
Paediatric dosing = always weight-basedNO. BSA-based for chemo (Mosteller); Age-based approximation only when weight unavailable.
Protein binding displacement = major interaction in elderlyRarely. Only for high-extraction, narrow TI drugs (warfarin, phenytoin, valproate).
Mnemonic: SPECIAL POPULATIONS
Special Populations: Renal, Hepatic, Pregnancy, Elderly, Paediatrics
Renal: Cockcroft-Gault for dosing; Metformin <30 = NO; DOACs adjust by eGFR; Aminoglycosides post-HD
Hepatic: Child-Pugh A/B/C; High-extraction (Propranolol, Morphine) vs Low (Warfarin, Phenytoin)
Pregnancy: Teratogenic Critical periods (NT 18-28d, Heart 20-50d, Limbs 24-46d); Valproate (NTD, Autism), Lithium (Ebstein), Warfarin (Embryopathy), ACEi (Renal dysplasia)
Elderly: STOPP/START; Beers; ACB; DBI; Depresc ribing (PPI, Benzo, Anticholinergic, Statin, HTN)
Paediatrics: Weight-based (mg/kg) most; BSA (Mosteller) for chemo; Young's Rule (Age+4)/24 × Adult
Breastfeeding: LactMed; Hale's; Safe = Short-acting, low milk transfer, poor oral bioavailability
Mnemonic: RENAL DOSING — "MADD-AB-VAC"
Metformin <30 NO, 30-45 max 1g BD
ACEi/ARB: monitor K, Cr; reduce if <30
DOACs: Riva 15mg (30-49), Apx 2.5mg BD (15-29), Dab 110mg BD (30-49); <15 Warfarin
DABIGATRAN: 110mg BD (CrCl 30-49), 75mg BD (15-29), avoid <15
Aminoglycosides: Extended interval; post-HD dosing
Benzenes (Vancomycin): Standard dose, AUC-guided; post-HD 15-20mg/kg
Vanc_mycin: AUC-guided; post-HD 15-20mg/kg
Allopurinol: 100mg max (<30), post-HD
Cholchicine: 0.5mg OD (30-59), alt days (15-29), avoid <15
Mnemonic: HEPATIC — "CHILD-PUGH"
Class A (5-6): Mild reduction high-extraction
High-extraction drugs: Propranolol, Lidocaine, Morphine, Verapamil, Nitrates
Intermediate (B: 7-9): 50% high, 25% low extraction reduction
Low-extraction: Warfarin, Phenytoin, Diazepam, Theophylline

+ Dose reduction severe (C: 10-15): Avoid most hepatically cleared

Pregnancy: ACEi/ARB Contraindicated 2nd/3rd tri
Uterine teratogens: Valproate, Warfarin, ACEi, Lithium, Isotretinoin, Methotrexate, Statins
Generally safe: Lamotrigine, Levetiracetam, Labetalol, Methyldopa, Nifedipine, LMWH, Insulin, Sertraline, Paracetamol

15. 15. 💬 Exam Answer Modes

FormatPromptKey Points
Long Essay"Describe the principles of prescribing in renal impairment, hepatic impairment, and pregnancy."Renal: CrCl (C-G), eGFR thresholds, metformin/DOAC/aminoglycoside adjustments. Hepatic: Child-Pugh A/B/C, high vs low extraction drugs. Pregnancy: FDA/UK categories, teratogens (valproate, warfarin, ACEi, lithium), safe drugs, teratogenic critical periods.
Short Note"Metformin in renal impairment — dosing and contraindication."eGFR ≥45: full dose; 30-44: max 1g BD; 15-29: contraindicated; <15/dialysis: contraindicated. Lactic acidosis risk.
Viva"32F, 12 weeks pregnant, epilepsy on valproate. Management?"Switch to lamotrigine/levetiracetam (lowest teratogenicity). Folic acid 5mg daily. Valproate: NTD 1-2%, developmental delay, autism.
Ward Round"85F, eGFR 28, on metformin 1g BD. Management?"Reduce to 500mg BD (max 1g/day if eGFR 30-45). Monitor lactate, renal function. If eGFR drops <30 → stop.
Last-Night"Renal: CG for dosing, Metformin <30 NO, 30-45 max 1g, DOACs eGFR adjust, Amino post-HD. Hepatic: Child-Pugh A/B/C, High-extraction (Propranolol, Morphine) vs Low (Warfarin, Phenytoin). Preg: Valproate/Lithium/Warfarin/ACEi teratogenic; Safe: Labetalol, LMWH, Insulin, Lamotrigine, Sertraline. Teratogenic periods: NT 18-28d, Heart 20-50d, Limbs 24-46d. Elderly: STOPP/START, Beers, ACB≥3=High risk. Paeds: Weight mg/kg, BSA Mosteller chemo. Elderly: STOPP/START, Deprescribing."Compressed.

16. 16. 📌 Summary

  • Renal Dosing: Cockcroft-Gault (CrCl) for dosing; eGFR thresholds: Metformin <30 contraindicated, 30-44 max 1g BD; DOACs adjust by eGFR; Aminoglycosides post-HD; Vancomycin AUC-guided.
  • Hepatic Dosing: Child-Pugh A/B/C; High-extraction (Propranolol, Lidocaine, Morphine, Verapamil, Nitrates) — reduce in liver disease; Low-extraction (Warfarin, Phenytoin, Diazepam, Theophylline) — adjust for enzyme capacity.
  • Pregnancy: Teratogens: Valproate (NTD, autism), Warfarin (embryopathy), ACEi/ARB (renal dysplasia), Lithium (Ebstein), Isotretinoin, Methotrexate, Statins. Safe: Labetalol, Methyldopa, Nifedipine, LMWH, Insulin, Lamotrigine, Levetiracetam, Sertraline, Paracetamol. Critical periods: Neural tube 18-28d, Heart 20-50d, Limbs 24-46d, Palate 42-56d.
  • Elderly: STOPP/START v3, Beers 2023, ACB/DBI/MAI, Deprescribing (PPI, Benzo, Anticholinergic, Statin, HTN).
  • Paediatrics: Weight-based (mg/kg) most; BSA (Mosteller) for chemo; Young's Rule (Age+4)/24 × Adult dose.
  • Contraindicated in Pregnancy (X): Isotretinoin, Thalidomide, Methotrexate, Ribavirin, Mycophenolate.
  • Lactation: Prefer short-acting, low milk transfer, poor oral bioavailability drugs; Consult LactMed/Hale's.
  • Renal Replacement: HD — post-HD dosing for renally cleared drugs; PD — similar but less drug removal; CRRT — higher clearance, adjust accordingly.

18. 18. 📋 SBAs (5)

  1. 85F, eGFR 28, T2DM on metformin 1g BD. Management?

A. Continue 1g BD

B. Reduce to 500mg BD (max 1g/day if eGFR 30-45); contraindicated if <30

C. Switch to gliclazide

D. Stop metformin

Answer: B

  1. 32F, 12 weeks pregnant, epilepsy on valproate 1g BD. Folic acid 5mg prescribed. Best AED switch?

A. Carbamazepine

B. Lamotrigine or Levetiracetam (lowest teratogenicity); Folic acid 5mg daily

C. Phenytoin

D. Topiramate

Answer: B

  1. 70M, Child-Pugh B cirrhosis, fractured hip. Safe opioid?

A. Morphine (standard dose)

B. Fentanyl/Alfentanil (low hepatic extraction, safe in liver disease); avoid morphine/codeine

C. Codeine 60mg

D. Tramadol 100mg

Answer: B

  1. 5-year-old, 18kg, amoxicillin for pneumonia. Dose?

A. 125mg TDS

B. 25mg/kg/dose = 450mg TDS (50mg/kg/day)

C. 500mg TDS

D. 250mg BD

Answer: B

  1. 85F, frail, 12 medications including lorazepam, digoxin, furosemide. STOPP?

A. Statin primary prevention >85y

B. Benzodiazepine >4w (falls, cognitive impairment) — STOPP B2

C. PPI >8w

D. Warfarin >1y VTE

Answer: B

MCQsSBAs
1-C, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B, 6-B, 7-B, 8-B, 9-B, 10-B1-B, 2-B, 3-B, 4-B, 5-B

topics.title: Aminoglycosides
topics.html:
Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring → Drug-Specific TDM | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (Daily ward/ITU practice, FCPS/MRCP staple)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Compare traditional vs extended-interval dosing (once-daily)
  • [ ] Apply TDM targets: peak/trough for traditional, Hartford nomogram for extended-interval
  • [ ] Calculate dose adjustments based on CrCl
  • [ ] Identify nephrotoxicity + ototoxicity risk factors
  • [ ] Execute monitoring protocol

2. 2. 📊 Dosing Strategies

ParameterTraditional (Multiple Daily)Extended-Interval (Once-Daily/Hartford)
DosingEvery 8–12h (3–4×/day)Once daily (q24h)
Peak TargetGentamicin: 5–8 mg/L (Amikacin: 15–25)Not routinely measured
Trough TargetGentamicin: <1–2 mg/L (Amikacin: <5–10)<1 mg/L at 18–24h post-dose
AUC Target—AUC 70–100 mg·h/L (Gentamicin)
AdvantagesFamiliar stewardship↓ Nephrotoxicity, ↓ Ototoxicity, convenience, cost
Contraindications—Pregnancy, Burns >20%, Ascites, ARC (CrCl>150), Synergistic therapy (Endocarditis)
Nephrotoxicity RiskHigher (sustained trough)Lower (low trough)
Post-Antibiotic Effect (PAE)Utilised but trough limitsMaximised (high peak, long drug-free interval)

3. 3. 🧮 Hartford Nomogram (Extended-Interval Gentamicin)

StepAction
1. Calculate CrClCockcroft-Gault (actual body weight unless obese → IBW + 0.4×(ABW-IBW))
2. Calculate Dose5–7 mg/kg IBW (traditional 7 mg/kg; obese use adjusted body weight)
3. AdministerIV infusion over 30–60 min
4. Obtain LevelsSingle level at 6–14h post-dose (ideally 10h)
5. Plot on Hartford NomogramX-axis = time post-dose (h); Y-axis = concentration (mg/L)
6. Determine Interval<2 mg/L at 6h → q24h; 2–4 mg/L at 10h → q24h; >4 mg/L at 10h → extend to q36h or q48h; >8 mg/L at 14h → q48h+
⬇ Clinical Pathway
Extended-Interval Gentamicin
↓
Dose: 5-7 mg/kg IBW q24h IV 30-60min
↓
Check level at 6-14h post-dose
↓
Level at 10h?
↓
q24h
q36h
q48h+ or switch to traditional

4. 4. 📏 Traditional Dosing TDM (If Used)

TimingSampleTarget
TroughJust before next doseGentamicin: <1–2 mg/L; Amikacin: <5–10 mg/L
Peak30 min post-IV infusionGentamicin: 5–8 mg/L; Amikacin: 15–25 mg/L
RandomAny timeUse Sawchuk-Zaske or Bayesian for prediction

5. 5. ⚖️ Renal Adjustment — CrCl-Based

CrCl (mL/min)Gentamicin TraditionalGentamicin ExtendedAmikacin Traditional
>601.5–2 mg/kg q8h7 mg/kg q24h15 mg/kg q8h
40–601.5 mg/kg q8–12h5–7 mg/kg q36h7.5–10 mg/kg q8–12h
20–401–1.5 mg/kg q12–24h5 mg/kg q48h7.5 mg/kg q12–24h
10–201 mg/kg q24–48hAVOID (use traditional)5–7.5 mg/kg q24–48h
<10 / HD1 mg/kg post-HDAVOID5–7.5 mg/kg post-HD
CrCl estimation: Use Cockcroft-Gault with IBW for aminoglycosides (not eGFR)

6. 6. ☠️ Toxicity

ToxicityMechanismRisk FactorsMonitoring
Nephrotoxicity (ATN)Proximal tubular phospholipidosis, ROSPre-existing CKD, Age>60, Dehydration, Loop diuretics, Vancomycin, Amphotericin B, Contrast, Long duration (>7d), High troughSCr, CrCl q2–3d; ↑ SCr ≥25% or ≥26.5 μmol/L = hold
Vestibular OtotoxicityVestibular hair cell damage (irreversible)High cumulative dose, Renal impairment, Elderly, Pre-existing vestibular diseaseVestibular symptoms (vertigo, imbalance, oscillopsia) — ask weekly
Cochlear OtotoxicityCochlear hair cell damage → high-frequency hearing loss (may be reversible early)High peak levels, Genetic predisposition (MT-RNR1 m.1555A>G)Audiometry baseline + if prolonged

7. 7. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

PearlDetails
Dosing strategyExtended-interval (once-daily) preferred — ↓ nephrotoxicity, ↓ ototoxicity, convenience
Hartford nomogramSingle level at 6–14h post-dose → determine q24h/q36h/q48h
Trough target (extended)<1 mg/L at 18–24h post-dose
Renal adjustmentCockcroft-Gault with IBW — not eGFR
Nephrotoxicity monitoringSCr q2–3d; ↑ SCr ≥25% → hold
OtotoxicityAsk about dizziness, hearing changes weekly; audiometry if prolonged
DurationLimit to 7–10 days if possible
Contraindications to extended-intervalPregnancy, Burns >20%, Ascites, ARC (CrCl>150), Endocarditis (synergistic)
Synergy (Endocarditis)Traditional dosing required for sustained bactericidal levels

8. 8. ❓ Viva Questions (10)

QAnswer
1. Extended-interval vs traditional gentamicin — advantages?↓ Nephrotoxicity, ↓ Ototoxicity, convenience, cost, utilises PAE; peak-driven bactericidal
2. Hartford nomogram — when to sample?6–14h post-dose (ideally 10h); single level
3. Extended-interval trough target?<1 mg/L at 18–24h post-dose
4. Extended-interval contraindications?Pregnancy, Burns >20%, Ascites, ARC (CrCl>150), Endocarditis (synergistic)
5. CrCl estimation for aminoglycosides?Cockcroft-Gault with IBW (not eGFR)
6. Nephrotoxicity — risk factors?CKD, Age>60, Dehydration, Loop diuretics, Vancomycin, Amphotericin, Contrast, >7d duration, High trough
7. Ototoxicity types — difference?Vestibular: vertigo, imbalance, irreversible; Cochlear: high-frequency hearing loss, may be partially reversible
8. Ototoxicity — monitoring?Ask weekly: dizziness, hearing changes; Audiometry if prolonged >14d
9. Extended-interval — when to extend interval per Hartford?Level >4 mg/L at 10h → q36h; >8 mg/L at 14h → q48h+
10. Aminoglycoside + Vancomycin — interaction?Synergistic nephrotoxicity — avoid if possible; if unavoidable, close monitoring

9. 9. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Extended-interval = always better?No — contraindicated in pregnancy, burns, ascites, ARC, endocarditis
CrCl vs eGFRUse Cockcroft-Gault CrCl with IBW for aminoglycosides
Trough vs PeakExtended = trough-driven; Traditional = peak + trough
Vestibular vs CochlearVestibular = balance (irreversible); Cochlear = hearing (some reversibility)
Hartford nomogram timeSample at 6–14h (ideally 10h) — not immediately post-dose

Mnemonics:

  • "EXTENDED = BETTER TOXICITY" = ↓ Nephrotoxicity, ↓ Ototoxicity
  • "HARTFORD" = Hartford: Half-day (10h) sample, Adjust interval (24/36/48h), Renal adjustment (CrCl), Trough <1 mg/L, Forbid in pregnancy/burns/ascites/ARC, Once daily, Review nephrotoxicity, Duration limit 7–10d
  • "NEPHROTOX RISK" = Nephrotoxicity ↑ with: Elderly, Pre-existing CKD, High trough, Renal drugs (loop, vanco, ampho, contrast), Over 7 days, Toxicity
  • "OTO = VESTIBULAR + COCHLEAR" = Vestibular = balance/vertigo (irreversible); Cochlear = hearing (partial reversibility)

10. 10. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Aminoglycosides TDM
Dosing Strategies
Traditional
– q8-12h
– Peak 5-8 / Trough <2
Extended (Once-Daily) PREFERRED
– q24h
– Hartford nomogram
– Trough <1 at 18-24h
– Peak NOT routine
Hartford Nomogram
Dose 7 mg/kg IBW
Level at 6-14h
<2 at 10h → q24h
2-4 at 10h → q24h
4-8 at 10h → q36h
>8 at 14h → q48h+
Contraindications (Extended)
Pregnancy
Burns >20%
Ascites
ARC (CrCl>150)
Endocarditis (synergy)
Renal Adjustment
Cockcroft-Gault + IBW
CrCl >60 → q24h
CrCl 40-60 → q36h
CrCl 20-40 → q48h
CrCl <20 → traditional only
Toxicity
Nephrotoxicity (ATN)

– Cochlear (hearing loss, partial rev)
– Ask weekly / Audiometry

11. 11. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScoreNext
11d
23d
31w
42w
51m
63m

12. 12. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore
Dosing comparison8
Hartford nomogram8
Contraindications6
Renal adjustment6
Toxicity8
Viva answers10
Total46

Target: ≥37/46 (80%)

13. 13. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Short Question (5 marks): "Extended-interval gentamicin — advantages, monitoring, contraindications."

  • Advantages: ↓ Nephrotoxicity (low trough), ↓ Ototoxicity, convenience, cost, utilises PAE
  • Monitoring: Hartford nomogram — single level at 6–14h post-dose (ideally 10h); target trough <1 mg/L at 18–24h; SCr q2–3d; ask vestibular symptoms weekly
  • Contraindications: Pregnancy, Burns >20%, Ascites, ARC (CrCl>150), Endocarditis (synergistic)

2. Viva (1 min): "Patient on extended-interval gentamicin. Level at 10h = 6 mg/L. Action?"

  • Level 6 mg/L at 10h → 4–8 mg/L zone → extend interval to q36h
  • Recheck level next dose at 10h
  • Monitor SCr q2–3d

3. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on gentamicin 7 days, SCr rising. Action?"

  • Nephrotoxicity (risk ↑ with duration >7d)
  • Hold gentamicin, switch to non-nephrotoxic alternative
  • Hydration, avoid other nephrotoxins
  • If must continue → switch to traditional monitoring

4. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Extended-interval = preferred (↓ nephro/ototoxicity); Hartford nomogram
  • Hartford: dose 7 mg/kg IBW q24h, level at 10h, <2=q24h, 2-4=q24h, 4-8=q36h, >8=q48h+
  • Contraindicated: Pregnancy, Burns>20%, Ascites, ARC, Endocarditis
  • CrCl = Cockcroft-Gault + IBW (not eGFR)
  • Nephrotoxicity: monitor SCr q2-3d, hold if ↑≥25%
  • Ototoxicity: vestibular (irreversible), cochlear (hearing); ask weekly

14. 14. 📚 Summary Card

AMINOGLYCOSIDES TDM:
EXTENDED-INTERVAL PREFERRED — Hartford nomogram
Dose: 7 mg/kg IBW q24h → Level at 10h
<2 → q24h; 2-4 → q24h; 4-8 → q36h; >8 → q48h+
Trough target: <1 mg/L at 18-24h
Contraindicated: Pregnancy, Burns>20%, Ascites, ARC, Endocarditis
Renal adjust: Cockcroft-Gault + IBW
Monitor: SCr q2-3d, Vestibular symptoms weekly

15. 15. ❓ MCQs (12)

  1. Extended-interval gentamicin dosing — major advantage over traditional:

A. Lower peak levels

B. Lower nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity ✓

C. Lower cost only

D. More convenient only

E. Higher trough levels

  1. Hartford nomogram — when to draw level:

A. Immediately post-dose

B. 6–14h post-dose (ideally 10h) ✓

C. Trough (pre-dose)

D. 30 min post-dose

E. 2h post-dose

  1. Extended-interval gentamicin trough target:

A. <2 mg/L

B. <1 mg/L at 18–24h post-dose ✓

C. <5 mg/L

D. 5–8 mg/L

E. No trough needed

  1. Extended-interval gentamicin — contraindicated in:

A. CKD

B. Pregnancy, Burns >20%, Ascites, ARC (CrCl>150), Endocarditis ✓

C. Elderly

D. Sepsis

E. Pneumonia

  1. CrCl estimation for aminoglycoside dosing:

A. eGFR (CKD-EPI)

B. Cockcroft-Gault with IBW ✓

C. MDRD

D. BUN/Cr ratio

E. 24h urine CrCl only

  1. Gentamicin level at 10h = 6 mg/L on extended-interval dosing — action:

A. Continue q24h

B. Extend to q36h ✓

C. Extend to q48h

D. Switch to traditional

E. Increase dose

  1. Gentamicin level at 14h = 10 mg/L — action:

A. Continue q24h

B. Extend to q36h

C. Extend to ≥q48h or switch to traditional ✓

D. Increase dose

E. No change

  1. Aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity — monitoring:

A. SCr weekly

B. SCr q2–3d ✓

C. SCr monthly

D. BUN only

E. UOP only

  1. Ototoxicity — vestibular vs cochlear:

A. Both reversible

B. Vestibular irreversible; Cochlear partially reversible ✓

C. Both irreversible

D. Vestibular reversible; Cochlear irreversible

E. Cochlear only

  1. Aminoglycoside + Vancomycin interaction:

A. Additive antibacterial

B. Synergistic nephrotoxicity ✓

C. Antagonistic

D. No interaction

E. Ototoxicity only

  1. Extended-interval dosing contraindicated in endocarditis because:

A. Poor penetration

B. Synergistic therapy requires sustained bactericidal levels (traditional) ✓

C. Higher nephrotoxicity

D. Drug resistance

E. Cost

  1. Aminoglycoside duration — limit if possible to:

A. 3–5 days

B. 7–10 days ✓

C. 14–21 days

D. 28 days

E. No limit

16. 16. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack

17. 17. ✅ Answer Keys

topics.title: Anticonvulsants
topics.html:
Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring → Drug-Specific TDM | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (Epilepsy, status epilepticus, pregnancy, interactions)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State therapeutic ranges for key anticonvulsants (Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Valproate, Phenobarbital, Lamotrigine, Levetiracetam, Topiramate)
  • [ ] Apply FREE phenytoin/valproate monitoring in hypoalbuminaemia/renal failure
  • [ ] Recognise toxicity features for each drug
  • [ ] Manage key drug interactions (enzyme induction/inhibition)
  • [ ] Apply dosing in pregnancy, renal/hepatic impairment

2. 2. 📊 Therapeutic Ranges & Sampling

DrugTherapeutic Range (µg/mL)Toxic ThresholdSampling
Phenytoin10–20 (Total) / 1–2 (Free)>20 (Total) / >2 (Free)Trough (pre-dose); steady state 5–10d (t½ 6–24h, dose-dep)
Carbamazepine4–12>12–15Trough (pre-dose); steady state 2–4wks (auto-induction)
Valproate50–100 (Total) / 5–10 (Free)>100 (Total) / >10 (Free)Trough (pre-dose); steady state 2–4d (t½ 9–16h)
Phenobarbital10–40>40–50Trough; steady state 2–3wks
Lamotrigine3–15 (no fixed range)>15–20Trough; steady state 5–7d
LevetiracetamNot routinely monitored (12–46 typical)No definedNot routine; if needed: trough
TopiramateNot routinely monitored (5–20 typical)No definedNot routine
Ethosuximide40–100>100Trough
Zonisamide10–40>40Trough
Free drug monitoring ESSENTIAL for: Phenytoin and Valproate in hypoalbuminaemia, renal failure, pregnancy, elderly, displacement interactions

3. 3. 🧪 Free vs Total Drug — Phenytoin & Valproate

1. Phenytoin

ParameterTotalFree
Protein Binding~90% bound to albumin~10% free
Therapeutic Range10–20 µg/mL1–2 µg/mL
When to Use FreeNormal albumin, no displacementHypoalbuminaemia (<35g/L), Renal failure (uraemia displaces), Pregnancy, Elderly, Valproate co-administration, Nephrotic syndrome, Burns

| Estimation (if free not available) | Corrected Total = Measured Total / (0.2 × Albumin + 0.1)

Sheiner-Tozer (renal): Corrected = Measured / (0.1 × Albumin + 0.1) | Direct assay preferred |

2. Valproate

ParameterTotalFree
Protein Binding~90% bound (dose-dependent: ↓ binding at higher levels)~10% free (increases with dose)
Therapeutic Range50–100 µg/mL5–10 µg/mL
When to Use FreeNormal albumin, low doseHypoalbuminaemia, Renal failure, Pregnancy, High dose (>1000mg/day), Elderly, Displacement (e.g., aspirin)
EstimationNot reliable due to saturable bindingDirect assay preferred

4. 4. ☠️ Toxicity Profiles

DrugAcute ToxicityChronic Toxicity
PhenytoinNystagmus, Ataxia, Dysarthria (cerebellar), Confusion, Coma, Bidirectional VT (rare), Hypotension (IV)Gingival hyperplasia, Hirsutism, Coarsening features, Osteomalacia (↓ Vit D), Neuropathy, Folate deficiency, Lymphadenopathy
CarbamazepineDizziness, Ataxia, N/V, Diplopia, Hyponatraemia (SIADH), Agranulocytosis (rare)Hyponatraemia (SIADH), Autoinduction (↓ levels over weeks), Enzyme induction (↓ other drugs), Aplastic anaemia (rare), Skin reactions (SCARs)
ValproateConfusion, Hyperammonaemic encephalopathy (normal LFTs!), Somnolence, Cerebral oedemaWeight gain, Tremor, Alopecia, PCOS, Thrombocytopenia (dose-dep), Hepatotoxicity (rare, <2y, polytherapy), Neural tube defects (teratogen)
PhenobarbitalSedation, Respiratory depression, Hypotension, AtaxiaEnzyme induction, Osteomalacia, Cognitive impairment, Dependence
LamotrigineRash (↑ with rapid titration/valproate), SJS/TEN, Aseptic meningitisRash, Headache, Insomnia
LevetiracetamSedation, Behavioural (aggression, psychosis, irritability), DizzinessRare chronic toxicity

5. 5. 🔄 Key Interactions (Enzyme Induction/Inhibition)

DrugInduces (↓ levels of others)Inhibited By (↑ levels)Inhibits (↑ levels of others)
PhenytoinCYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19, 1A2, UGTValproate, Fluconazole, Amiodarone, Cimetidine, Sulfonamides, Chloramphenicol, Disulfiram, Isoniazid, FelbamateWarfarin, DOACs, OCP, Corticosteroids, Theophylline, Ciclosporin, Tacrolimus
CarbamazepineCYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19, 1A2, UGT, P-gpValproate, Fluconazole, Erythromycin, Clarithromycin, Verapamil, Diltiazem, Propoxyphene, Fluvoxamine, NefazodoneWarfarin, DOACs, OCP, Ciclosporin, Tacrolimus, Sirolimus
PhenobarbitalCYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19, 1A2, UGT, P-gpValproate, FelbamateWarfarin, DOACs, OCP, Ciclosporin, Tacrolimus
ValproateUGT (weak)Carbamazepine (epoxide), RifampicinPhenytoin (displace + inhibit), Carbamazepine (epoxide), Lamotrigine (↑ 2x), Phenobarbital, Topiramate
Lamotrigine—Valproate (↑ 2x), OCP (↓ levels)—
LevetiracetamNoneNoneNone (no CYP interaction)
TopiramateCYP3A4 (weak, high dose)ValproateOCP (↓ ethinylestradiol at ≥200mg)

6. 6. ⚖️ Special Populations

1. Pregnancy

DrugTeratogenicityDose AdjustmentFolic Acid
ValproateHIGHEST — Neural tube defects (1–2%), Congenital malformations (10%), Neurodevelopmental delay (ADHD, ASD)AVOID IN PREGNANCY; if essential: lowest effective dose, monitor free levels q4w5mg/day pre-conception
CarbamazepineNeural tube defects (0.5–1%), Craniofacial, CardiacFree levels ↓ in pregnancy — ↑ dose 25–50%, monitor free levels q4w5mg/day
PhenytoinFetal hydantoin syndrome (craniofacial, limb, cardiac), NTDsFree levels ↓ — monitor free levels q4w; ↑ dose often needed5mg/day
LamotrigineLow risk (NTDs ~2–3% at high dose); clearance ↑ 2–3x in pregnancyLevels ↓ dramatically — ↑ dose 50–100%, monitor free levels q4w5mg/day
LevetiracetamLowest risk (preferred in pregnancy)Clearance ↑ — ↑ dose often needed5mg/day
TopiramateCleft lip/palate, NTDs, Low birth weight, ↓ fetal growthMonitor levels5mg/day

2. Renal Impairment

DrugRenal ExcretionAdjustment
Levetiracetam~66% renal (unchanged)↓ Dose by 50% if CrCl<50; avoid if CrCl<30 (or specialist)
Gabapentin100% renalCrCl-based dosing (e.g., CrCl 30–60: 300mg BD; 15–30: 300mg daily; <15: 300mg alt day)
Pregabalin~98% renalSimilar to gabapentin
Topiramate~50% renal↓ Dose if CrCl<50
Zonisamide~35% renal↓ Dose if CrCl<50
Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Valproate, PhenobarbitalMinimal renalNo renal adjustment; monitor FREE levels in uraemia

3. Hepatic Impairment

DrugHepatic MetabolismAdjustment
PhenytoinCYP2C9/2C19Use FREE levels; ↓ dose if severe
CarbamazepineCYP3A4Use FREE levels; ↓ dose if severe
ValproateUGT, β-oxidationCONTRAINDICATED in severe hepatic impairment; avoid in active liver disease
LamotrigineUGT1A4↓ Dose if severe
LevetiracetamMinimalNo adjustment

7. 7. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

PearlDetails
Phenytoin free level1–2 µg/mL (total 10–20); use in hypoalbuminaemia, uraemia, pregnancy, valproate co-admin
Valproate free level5–10 µg/mL (total 50–100); use in hypoalbuminaemia, renal failure, pregnancy, high dose
CarbamazepineAutoinduction — levels drop over 2–4 weeks; SIADH common; HLA-B*15:02 screen (Asians)
Valproate toxicityHyperammonaemic encephalopathy (normal LFTs, ↑ ammonia → L-carnitine 100mg/kg); Hepatotoxicity (<2y, polytherapy)
Phenytoin toxicityCerebellar: nystagmus, ataxia, dysarthria (level >20)
LevetiracetamNo CYP interactions; behavioural toxicity (aggression, psychosis); renal adjustment
Lamotrigine + ValproateValproate ↑ Lamotrigine 2x — ↓ lamotrigine dose, slow titration
PregnancyValproate CONTRAINDICATED; Lamotrigine/Levetiracetam preferred; Folic acid 5mg/day; monitor free levels q4w
RenalLevetiracetam, Gabapentin, Pregabalin = renal adjustment; Phenytoin/Carbamazepine/Valproate = free levels

8. 8. ❓ Viva Questions (12)

QAnswer
1. Phenytoin free therapeutic range? When to use?1–2 µg/mL; use in hypoalbuminaemia, renal failure (uraemia), pregnancy, elderly, valproate co-administration, displacement interactions
2. Phenytoin corrected total formula (Sheiner-Tozer for renal)?Corrected = Measured / (0.1 × Albumin + 0.1)
3. Valproate free therapeutic range? When to use?5–10 µg/mL; use in hypoalbuminaemia, renal failure, pregnancy, high dose, elderly, displacement
4. Valproate hyperammonaemic encephalopathy — mechanism, treatment?Inhibits carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I → urea cycle dysfunction → ↑ ammonia; L-carnitine 100mg/kg IV; stop valproate
5. Carbamazepine autoinduction — clinical implication?Levels drop over 2–4 weeks; need dose escalation; ↓ levels of other drugs (OCP, warfarin, etc.)

9. 9. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Free vs Total phenytoinFree = 1–2 µg/mL (10% of total); use free in hypoalbuminaemia, uraemia, pregnancy
Valproate protein bindingSaturable — free fraction ↑ at higher doses (>1000mg/day)
Lamotrigine + ValproateValproate inhibits lamotrigine glucuronidation → ↑ levels 2x
Levetiracetam interactionsNONE — no CYP, no protein binding, renal excretion
Pregnancy lamotrigineClearance ↑ 2–3x → levels plummet → need 50–100% dose increase
Carbamazepine HLA-B*15:02Screen in Asian ancestry before starting (SJS/TEN risk)

Mnemonics:

  • "FREE PHENYTOIN = 1-2" = use in hypoalbuminaemia, uraemia, pregnancy, valproate
  • "FREE VALPROATE = 5-10" = use in hypoalbuminaemia, renal failure, pregnancy, high dose
  • "VALPROATE AMMONIA" = inhibits CPS1 → urea cycle → ↑ ammonia → L-carnitine 100mg/kg
  • "CARBAMAZEPINE AUTO" = autoinduction → levels drop 2-4wks
  • "LAMOTRIGINE + VALPROATE" = valproate ↑ lamotrigine 2x → slow titrate, ↓ dose
  • "LEVETIRACETAM = NO CYP" = clean interaction profile; renal adjust
  • "PREGNANCY AED" = Valproate NO; Carbamazepine/Phenytoin = monitor free; Lamotrigine/Levetiracetam = preferred; Folate 5mg
  • "RENAL AED" = Levetiracetam/Gabapentin/Pregabalin = renal adjust; others = free levels

13. 13. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Short Question (5 marks): "Phenytoin free drug monitoring — when, range, formula."

  • Range: Free 1–2 µg/mL (Total 10–20)
  • When: Hypoalbuminaemia (<35), Renal failure/uraemia, Pregnancy, Elderly, Valproate co-administration, Displacement interactions
  • Formula (Sheiner-Tozer, renal failure): Corrected Total = Measured Total / (0.1 × Albumin + 0.1)
  • Standard formula: Corrected Total = Measured Total / (0.2 × Albumin + 0.1)

2. Viva (1 min): "Patient on valproate 1500mg/day, confused, drowsy. LFTs normal. Ammonia 180. Management?"

  • Valproate hyperammonaemic encephalopathy
  • STOP valproate immediately
  • L-carnitine 100mg/kg IV
  • Switch to alternative AED (levetiracetam, lamotrigine)
  • Monitor ammonia, consciousness

3. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on carbamazepine, started on clarithromycin. 1 week later: dizziness, ataxia. Why?"

  • Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 → ↓ carbamazepine metabolism → carbamazepine toxicity
  • STOP clarithromycin (switch to azithromycin if needed)
  • Monitor carbamazepine level; ↓ carbamazepine dose
  • Supportive care

4. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Phenytoin free = 1–2; Total = 10–20; use free in hypoalbuminaemia, uraemia, pregnancy
  • Valproate free = 5–10; Total = 50–100; saturable binding
  • Valproate encephalopathy = normal LFTs + ↑ ammonia → L-carnitine 100mg/kg
  • Carbamazepine autoinduction = levels drop 2–4wks; SIADH common; HLA-B*15:02 screen
  • Lamotrigine + Valproate = valproate ↑ lamotrigine 2x → slow titrate, ↓ dose
  • Levetiracetam = NO CYP interactions; renal adjust; behavioural toxicity
  • Pregnancy: Valproate NO; Folate 5mg; Lamotrigine/LEV preferred; free levels q4w
  • Renal: LEV, Gabapentin, Pregabalin = renal adjust

14. 14. 📚 Summary Card

ANTICONVULSANT TDM:
PHENYTOIN: Total 10-20, Free 1-2 (hypoalbuminaemia, uraemia, pregnancy)
VALPROATE: Total 50-100, Free 5-10 (saturable, hypoalbuminaemia, renal, pregnancy)
CARBAMAZEPINE: 4-12, Autoinduction, SIADH, HLA-B*15:02
VALPROATE ENCEPHALOPATHY: Normal LFTs + ↑ Ammonia → L-carnitine 100mg/kg
LAMOTRIGINE + VALPROATE: Valproate ↑ LMT 2x → ↓ dose, slow titrate
LEVETIRACETAM: No CYP, Renal adjust, Behavioural tox
PREGNANCY: Valproate NO; Folate 5mg; LMT/LEV preferred; Free q4w
RENAL: LEV/Gabapentin/Pregabalin = adjust; Others = free levels

topics.title: Digoxin
topics.html:
Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring → Drug-Specific TDM | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (AF, HF, narrow TI, toxicity classic)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State therapeutic range & toxic thresholds
  • [ ] Apply renal adjustment & P-gp interaction management
  • [ ] Recognise toxicity: ECG, clinical, biochemical
  • [ ] Execute Digoxin-specific Fab management
  • [ ] Apply sampling timing rules

2. 2. 📊 Therapeutic Range & Timing

IndicationTherapeutic Range (ng/mL)Toxic Threshold
Atrial Fibrillation (Rate Control)0.5–0.9 (some guidelines 0.5–1.0)>1.2–1.5
Heart Failure (HFrEF)0.5–0.9 (lower end preferred)>1.0 (no mortality benefit above 0.9)
Toxicity—>2.0 = severe toxicity
Key: No proven benefit of levels >0.9 ng/mL in HF; target 0.5–0.9 for both AF and HF

1. Sampling Timing — CRITICAL

SampleTimingRationale
Trough (pre-dose)≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h)Must be post-distribution phase (t½α ~1–2h)
RandomAny timeUse nomogram/software; NOT preferred
PeakNot usedNo clinical utility
If level drawn <6h post-dose: Falsely elevated (distribution phase) — REPEAT

3. 3. ⚖️ Renal Adjustment (CrCl-Based)

CrCl (mL/min)Dose AdjustmentMonitoring
>80Standard (0.125–0.25mg daily)Level at 5–7 days
50–800.125mg daily (↓ 25–50%)Level at 5–7 days
30–500.125mg daily or alternate dayLevel at 5–7 days
10–300.0625–0.125mg alternate dayLevel at 5–7 days
<10 / HD0.0625mg post-HD or alternate dayLevel pre-HD
Use Lean/IBW for Vd calculation; CrCl = Cockcroft-Gault (not eGFR)

4. 4. 🔄 Major Drug Interactions (↑ Digoxin Levels)

Drug / ClassMechanismDose Reduction
AmiodaroneP-gp inhibition + ↓ renal clearance + ↓ Vd↓ 30–50%
VerapamilP-gp inhibition↓ 30–50%
DiltiazemP-gp inhibition↓ 30–50%
QuinidineP-gp inhibition (most potent) + ↓ Vd↓ 50%
DronedaroneP-gp inhibition↓ 30–50%
Clarithromycin / Erythromycin / AzithromycinP-gp inhibition (clarithro > erythro > azithro)↓ 30–50%
ItraconazoleP-gp inhibition↓ 30–50%
Ciclosporin / TacrolimusP-gp inhibition↓ 30–50%
Spironolactone / Eplerenone↓ Renal clearance (creatinine secretion competition)Monitor K⁺, level
RifampicinP-gp INDUCTION → ↓ digoxin levelsMay need ↑ dose
St John's WortP-gp INDUCTIONMay need ↑ dose

5. 5. ☠️ Digoxin Toxicity

1. Clinical Features

SystemFeatures
Cardiac (Most Dangerous)Bradyarrhythmias (sinus brady, AV block — any degree), Ectopy (PVCs, VT, Bidirectional VT = pathognomonic), AF with slow ventricular response
GIAnorexia, Nausea, Vomiting (most common), Abdominal pain, Diarrhoea
NeurologicalFatigue, Weakness, Confusion, Visual disturbances (Yellow-green halos / xanthopsia), Headache, Dizziness
ElectrolyteHyperkalaemia (acute toxicity) — inhibits Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase → K⁺ not pumped into cells

2. ECG Findings (Classic)

FindingSignificance
"Scooped" / "Reverse tick" ST depression (salvador dalí sign)Therapeutic effect (not toxicity)
PR prolongation, AV block (1st, 2nd, 3rd degree)Toxicity
Bidirectional VTSevere toxicity (pathognomonic)
PVCs, Bigeminy, TrigeminyToxicity
Atrial tachycardia with blockClassic toxicity (paroxysmal atrial tachycardia + AV block)

6. 6. 💊 Management of Toxicity

SeverityAction
Mild (Level 1.5–2.0, asymptomatic)Hold digoxin; correct K⁺/Mg²⁺; recheck level in 24–48h
Moderate (Level >2.0, symptomatic: nausea, bradycardia, AV block)Hold digoxin; correct K⁺/Mg²⁺; Consider Digoxin-Specific Fab if persistent arrhythmia
Severe (Life-threatening arrhythmia, K⁺ >5.5, Ingestion >10mg, Level >4 ng/mL)Digoxin-Specific Fab (DigiFab)

1. Digoxin-Specific Fab (DigiFab) Dosing

ScenarioVials (40mg/vial)
Known ingested doseVials = (Ingested mg × 100) / 40 = Ingested mg × 2.5
Unknown ingested / Chronic toxicityVials = (Serum level ng/mL × Weight kg) / 100 (approx)
Empirical (Cardiac arrest / Life-threatening)10–20 vials
Each vial binds ~0.5 mg digoxin; Give IV over 30 min; Monitor K⁺ (Fab releases bound digoxin → can cause transient ↑ free digoxin); Recheck level 24h post-Fab (assay measures total digoxin = free + Fab-bound → falsely elevated)

7. 7. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

PearlDetails
Therapeutic range0.5–0.9 ng/mL (both AF & HF); >1.0 toxic in HF; >2.0 severe
SamplingTrough ≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h); <6h = falsely high
Renal adjustmentCrCl-based (Cockcroft-Gault); ↓ dose progressively
Top interactionsAmiodarone, Verapamil, Quinidine, Dronedarone, Macrolides, Itraconazole, Ciclosporin → ↓ dose 30–50%
Toxicity ECGAV block, Bidirectional VT, "Scooped" ST = therapeutic
HyperkalaemiaAcute toxicity (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition)
Digoxin FabLife-threatening arrhythmia, K⁺>5.5, Ingestion>10mg, Level>4
Rifampicin/St John's WortInducers → ↓ digoxin levels (may need ↑ dose)

8. 8. ❓ Viva Questions (10)

QAnswer
1. Digoxin therapeutic range for AF and HF?0.5–0.9 ng/mL for both; no benefit >0.9 in HF
2. When to sample digoxin level?Trough ≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h); <6h = falsely high (distribution)
3. Digoxin toxicity — pathognomonic ECG?Bidirectional ventricular tachycardia
4. Digoxin toxicity — "therapeutic" ECG?Scooped / reverse tick ST depression (Salvador Dalí sign) = therapeutic
5. Top 3 drugs that increase digoxin levels?Amiodarone, Verapamil, Quinidine (P-gp inhibition); also Dronedarone, Clarithromycin, Itraconazole
6. Rifampicin effect on digoxin?P-gp INDUCTION → ↓ digoxin levels (may need dose increase)
7. Hyperkalaemia in digoxin toxicity — mechanism?Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition → K⁺ not pumped into cells
8. Digoxin-Specific Fab indications?Life-threatening arrhythmia, K⁺>5.5, Ingestion>10mg, Level>4 ng/mL
9. Digoxin Fab dosing for known ingestion?Vials = Ingested mg × 2.5 (each vial binds 0.5 mg)
10. Renal adjustment — CrCl 30 mL/min?0.125mg daily or alternate day; monitor level

9. 9. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Scooped ST = toxicity?No — it's therapeutic effect (↑ Ca²⁺ in myocytes); toxicity = AV block, bidirectional VT
Hyperkalaemia = toxicityYes — acute toxicity (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition); chronic toxicity may have normal K⁺
Sampling <6hFalsely elevated — repeat at ≥6h
Rifampicin = ↓ digoxinInducer (P-gp) — opposite of most interactions
Digoxin level post-FabFalsely elevated (assay measures total = free + Fab-bound)

Mnemonics:

  • "DIGOXIN RANGE" = 0.5–0.9 (AF & HF); >1 toxic in HF; >2 severe
  • "SAMPLE AT 6-H+ HOURS" = 6h post-dose minimum for trough
  • "TOXICITY = BIDIRECTIONAL VT + AV BLOCK + HYPERKALAEMIA"
  • "SCOOPED ST = THERAPEUTIC" = Salvador Dalí moustache
  • "P-GP INHIBITORS = AMIODARONE, VERAPAMIL, QUINIDINE, DRONEDARONE, MACROLIDES, ITRACONAZOLE" → ↓ DIGOXIN 30-50%
  • "RIFAMPICIN = INDUCER = ↓ DIGOXIN"
  • "FAB INDICATIONS" = K>5.5, Ingestion>10mg, Level>4, Life-threatening arrhythmia
  • "FAB DOSE = INGESTED MG × 2.5" (each vial 40mg binds 0.5mg digoxin)

10. 10. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Digoxin TDM
Therapeutic Range
AF: 0.5-0.9
HF: 0.5-0.9 (no benefit >0.9)
Toxic: >1.0 (HF), >1.2-1.5 (AF), >2.0 severe
Sampling
Trough ≥6h post-dose (ideal 8-12h)
<6h = falsely high
Renal Adjustment
CrCl >80: standard
CrCl 50-80: 0.125mg daily
CrCl 30-50: 0.125mg daily/alt day
CrCl 10-30: 0.0625-0.125mg alt day
HD: 0.0625mg post-HD
P-gp Interactions (↑ Levels)
Amiodarone, Verapamil, Quinidine (↓50%)
Dronedarone, Clarithromycin, Itraconazole
Ciclosporin, Tacrolimus, Spironolactone
Inducers (↓ Levels)
Rifampicin, St John's Wort
Toxicity
Cardiac: AV block, Bidirectional VT, PVCs
GI: Nausea, Vomiting (common)
Neuro: Yellow-green halos (xanthopsia)
K+: Hyperkalaemia (acute)
ECG
Scooped ST = THERAPEUTIC
AV block / Bidirectional VT = TOXICITY
Fab (DigiFab)
Indications: K>5.5, Ingest>10mg, Level>4, Life-threatening arrhythmia
Dose: Ingested mg × 2.5 vials
Post-Fab level: falsely high (total = free + bound)

13. 13. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Short Question (5 marks): "Digoxin therapeutic range, sampling, and toxicity features."

  • Range: 0.5–0.9 ng/mL (AF & HF); >1.0 toxic (HF), >2.0 severe
  • Sampling: Trough ≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h); <6h falsely high
  • Toxicity: AV block, Bidirectional VT (pathognomonic), nausea/vomiting, yellow-green halos, hyperkalaemia (acute)
  • ECG: Scooped ST = therapeutic; AV block/bidirectional VT = toxicity

2. Viva (1 min): "Patient on digoxin + amiodarone for AF. Presents with nausea, bradycardia 45 bpm, K⁺ 5.8. Digoxin level 2.8. Management?"

  • Digoxin toxicity (amiodarone ↑ digoxin via P-gp inhibition)
  • Hold digoxin; correct K⁺/Mg²⁺
  • K⁺ 5.8 + bradycardia + level >2.0 = Digoxin-Specific Fab indicated
  • DigiFab: Ingested dose unknown → empirical 10–20 vials or level-based
  • Monitor K⁺ post-Fab (transient ↑); recheck level 24h (falsely high)

3. Ward Round (30 sec): "Digoxin level drawn 2h post-dose = 1.8 ng/mL. Interpretation?"

  • Falsely elevated — drawn in distribution phase (<6h)
  • Repeat at ≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h pre-dose)

4. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Digoxin range = 0.5–0.9 (AF & HF); no benefit >0.9 in HF
  • Sample trough ≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h)
  • Toxicity: AV block, Bidirectional VT, hyperkalaemia, yellow halos
  • Scooped ST = therapeutic (NOT toxicity)
  • P-gp inhibitors (Amiodarone, Verapamil, Quinidine) → ↓ digoxin 30–50%
  • Rifampicin/St John's Wort = inducers → ↓ digoxin
  • Digoxin Fab: K>5.5, Ingest>10mg, Level>4, Life-threatening arrhythmia
  • Fab dose = Ingested mg × 2.5

14. 14. 📚 Summary Card

DIGOXIN TDM:
RANGE 0.5-0.9 (AF & HF); >1 toxic in HF; >2 severe
TROUGH ≥6h POST-DOSE (distribution phase <6h = falsely high)
TOXICITY = AV block, BIDIRECTIONAL VT, K⁺↑, Yellow halos
SCOOPED ST = THERAPEUTIC
P-GP INHIBITORS (Amiodarone, Verapamil, Quinidine) → ↓ DIGOXIN 30-50%
FAB = K>5.5, Ingest>10mg, Level>4, Arrhythmia → Dose = Ingested mg × 2.5

16. 16. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
Digoxin range (AF & HF)0.5–0.9 ng/mL

Scooped ST depressionTherapeutic effect (NOT toxicity)
Hyperkalaemia mechanismNa/K-ATPase inhibition
P-gp inhibitors ↑ digoxinAmiodarone, Verapamil, Quinidine, Dronedarone, Macrolides, Itraconazole
QuinidineMost potent P-gp inhibitor (+ ↓ Vd) → ↓ digoxin 50%
Rifampicin/St John's WortP-gp INDUCERS → ↓ digoxin
Digoxin Fab indicationsK>5.5, Ingest>10mg, Level>4, Life-threatening arrhythmia
Digoxin Fab doseIngested mg × 2.5 vials (each binds 0.5mg)
Post-Fab levelFalsely elevated (total = free + bound)

17. 17. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — 0.5–0.9 ng/mL
  2. C — ≥6h post-dose (ideally 8–12h)
  3. C — Bidirectional VT
  4. B — Therapeutic effect
  5. B — ↓ 30–50%
  6. B — ↓ Levels (P-gp induction)
  7. B — Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase inhibition
  8. C — K⁺ >5.5 mmol/L
  9. C — Vials = Ingested mg × 2.5
  10. B — Falsely elevated (total = free + bound)
  11. B — 0.0625–0.125mg alternate day
  12. B — P-gp inhibition + ↓ Vd

topics.title: Immunosuppressants
topics.html:
Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring → Drug-Specific TDM | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (Transplant, life-long TDM, narrow TI, drug interactions)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State target trough/AUC ranges for Tacrolimus, Ciclosporin, Sirolimus, Everolimus, Mycophenolate
  • [ ] Apply CYP3A4/P-gp interaction management (azoles, macrolides, CCBs, rifampicin, etc.)
  • [ ] Recognise toxicity: nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, metabolic, infectious
  • [ ] Manage dose adjustments for renal/hepatic impairment
  • [ ] Execute monitoring protocols post-transplant

2. 2. 📊 Target Ranges & Pharmacokinetics

DrugTarget Trough (ng/mL)AUC TargetKey PK

| Tacrolimus (Tac) | Kidney: 5–15 (early 10–15, late 5–10)

Liver: 5–10

Heart/Lung: 10–15 | AUC 200–300 ng·h/mL (limited use) | CYP3A4/5 substrate

P-gp substrate

High Vd, low bioavailability (20–25%)

t½ ~12h |

| Ciclosporin (CsA) | Kidney: 100–300 (early 200–300, late 100–200)

Liver: 100–200

C2 monitoring (2h post): 800–1500 | AUC 3000–5000 ng·h/mL | CYP3A4/5 substrate

P-gp substrate

Low bioavailability (20–50%), variable

t½ ~6–12h |

| Sirolimus (SRL) | 4–12 (de novo kidney: 8–12; with CsA: 4–8) | AUC 100–200 ng·h/mL | CYP3A4/5 substrate

P-gp substrate

High Vd, low bioavailability (14%)

t½ ~60h |

| Everolimus (EVR) | 3–8 (with CsA: 3–5; with Tac: 6–10) | AUC 30–80 ng·h/mL | CYP3A4/5 substrate

P-gp substrate

t½ ~30h |

| Mycophenolate (MMF/MPA) | Not routinely trough (MPA trough 1–3.5 µg/mL) | AUC 30–60 mg·h/L (MPA) | UGT1A9 glucuronidation

Enterohepatic recycling

No CYP metabolism

Renal excretion of MPAG |

C2 monitoring (Ciclosporin): 2h post-dose level correlates better with AUC; used in some centres for de novo kidney/liver

3. 3. 🔄 Major CYP3A4/P-gp Interactions — CRITICAL

1. Strong Inhibitors (↑ Levels 2–10x) — REDUCE DOSE

InhibitorTac / CsA / SRL / EVR Dose AdjustmentNotes
Ketoconazole↓ 66–80% (Tac: divide by 3–5)Potent CYP3A4 + P-gp inhibitor
Itraconazole↓ 50–66%
Posaconazole↓ 50–66%
Voriconazole↓ 66–90% (Tac: reduce to 1/10–1/3 dose)Most potent — monitor levels daily
Fluconazole↓ 50%
Clarithromycin / Erythromycin↓ 50–66%Azithromycin safer (no CYP3A4 inhibition)
Verapamil / Diltiazem↓ 30–50%
Dronedarone↓ 50%
Amiodarone↓ 30–50%
Grapefruit Juice↓ 30–50%Intestinal CYP3A4 + P-gp inhibition
Protease Inhibitors (Ritonavir, etc.)↓ 80–90%Avoid if possible

2. Strong Inducers (↓ Levels 50–90%) — INCREASE DOSE / AVOID

InducerEffectManagement
Rifampicin↓ 80–90% (near complete loss)AVOID; if essential: ↑ dose 3–5x, monitor levels daily
Rifabutin↓ 50–80%↑ Dose, monitor
Carbamazepine / Phenytoin / Phenobarbital↓ 50–80%↑ Dose, monitor
St John's Wort↓ 50–80%AVOID
Dexamethasone (high dose)↓ 30–50%Monitor

4. 4. ☠️ Toxicity Profiles

DrugNephrotoxicityNeurotoxicityMetabolic / OtherInfection / Malignancy
TacrolimusAfferent arteriolar vasoconstriction → ↑ Cr, ↓ GFR (dose-dep, reversible)Tremor, Headache, Insomnia, Seizures, PRES (high levels)New-onset diabetes (PTDM) — 15–20%, Hyperkalaemia, Hypomagnesaemia, Hyperlipidaemia↑ CMV, BK virus, PTLD (EBV), Skin cancer
CiclosporinAfferent vasoconstriction → ↑ Cr (dose-dep); Thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA)Tremor, Hirsutism, Gingival hyperplasia, Seizures, PRESHyperlipidaemia, Hyperuricaemia/gout, Hypertension, Hyperkalaemia↑ CMV, BK, PTLD, Skin cancer
Sirolimus / EverolimusProteinuria (podocyte injury), ↓ Wound healing, LymphoceleOral ulcers, DysgeusiaHyperlipidaemia (↑ TG/Chol), Hyperglycaemia, Thrombocytopenia, Anaemia, Impaired wound healing, Pneumonitis (rare)↑ Infections (atypical), PTLD risk lower than CNI
MycophenolateRareRare (dizziness)GI (diarrhoea, N/V) — 30–40%, Bone marrow suppression (leukopenia, anaemia)↑ CMV, HSV, VZV; Teratogenic

5. 5. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

PearlDetails
Tacrolimus troughKidney: 5–15 (early 10–15, late 5–10); Liver: 5–10; Heart/Lung: 10–15
Ciclosporin troughKidney: 100–300 (early 200–300, late 100–200); C2: 800–1500
Sirolimus trough4–12 (higher if CNI-free)
Voriconazole + Tacrolimus↓ Tac dose to 1/10–1/3 (90% reduction); monitor levels daily
Azoles + CNIAll azoles ↓ CNI dose; Fluconazole ↓ 50%; Voriconazole ↓ 90%
Clarithromycin + CNI↓ CNI dose 50–66%; Use azithromycin instead
Rifampicin + CNI↓ Levels 80–90% — AVOID
Tacrolimus nephrotoxicityAfferent vasoconstriction; distinguish from rejection (biopsy)
Sirolimus proteinuriaPodocyte injury; switch to CNI if >1g/day
Mycophenolate GIDose reduction, divided doses, enteric-coated (mycophenolate sodium)
PTDM (Tacrolimus)New-onset diabetes — 15–20% kidney, 30% liver; monitor HbA1c, glucose

6. 6. ❓ Viva Questions (12)

QAnswer
1. Tacrolimus target trough for kidney transplant (early vs late)?Early (0–3mo): 10–15 ng/mL; Late (>3mo): 5–10 ng/mL
2. Ciclosporin target trough for kidney transplant?Early: 200–300 ng/mL; Late: 100–200 ng/mL; C2 (2h post): 800–1500 ng/mL
3. Sirolimus target trough?4–12 ng/mL (de novo 8–12; with CsA 4–8)
4. Voriconazole + Tacrolimus — dose adjustment?Reduce tacrolimus to 1/10–1/3 of dose (90% reduction); monitor levels daily
5. Fluconazole + Tacrolimus — dose adjustment?Reduce tacrolimus by 50%; monitor levels
6. Clarithromycin + Ciclosporin — dose adjustment?Reduce ciclosporin by 50–66%; prefer azithromycin
7. Rifampicin + Tacrolimus — effect?↓ Tacrolimus levels 80–90% (near complete loss) — AVOID
8. Tacrolimus vs Ciclosporin — key toxicity difference?Tac: PTDM (diabetes), tremor, neurotoxicity; CsA: Hirsutism, gingival hyperplasia, hyperlipidaemia, gout, hypertension, TMA
9. Sirolimus — unique toxicities?Proteinuria (podocyte), Impaired wound healing, Lymphocele, Hyperlipidaemia (TG/Chol), Pneumonitis, Oral ulcers
10. Mycophenolate — main toxicities?GI (diarrhoea, nausea — 30–40%), Bone marrow suppression (leukopenia, anaemia), Teratogenic
11. CNI nephrotoxicity — mechanism?Afferent arteriolar vasoconstriction → ↓ GFR; dose-dependent, reversible
12. PTDM — which CNI higher risk?Tacrolimus > Ciclosporin; 15–20% kidney, 30% liver

7. 7. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Tacrolimus trough 5–15 — what does it mean?Early post-Tx (0–3mo) = 10–15; Maintenance (>3mo) = 5–10
C2 monitoring2h post-dose CsA level — correlates better with AUC; used in some centres
Voriconazole interactionMost potent — Tac ↓ to 1/10–1/3 dose (90% reduction)
Azithromycin vs ClarithromycinAzithromycin = NO CYP3A4 inhibition — safe with CNI
Tacrolimus vs CsA diabetesTacrolimus >> CsA for new-onset diabetes (PTDM)
Sirolimus proteinuriaPodocyte injury — if >1g/day, consider switch to CNI
Mycophenolate + RifampicinRifampicin ↓ MPA AUC 30–50% (disrupts enterohepatic recycling)

Mnemonics:

  • "TAC RANGE" = Kidney: 10-15 early, 5-10 late; Liver: 5-10; Heart/Lung: 10-15
  • "CSA RANGE" = Kidney: 200-300 early, 100-200 late; C2: 800-1500
  • "SRL RANGE" = 4-12 (higher if CNI-free)
  • "VORI = 1/10 TAC" = Voriconazole → Tacrolimus dose 1/10–1/3
  • "FLUCONAZOLE = 1/2 TAC" = Fluconazole → Tacrolimus 50% dose
  • "CLARI = 1/2 CSA" = Clarithromycin → Ciclosporin 50-66%
  • "RIFA = AVOID CNI" = Rifampicin → ↓ CNI 80-90% — AVOID
  • "AZITHRO = SAFE" = Azithromycin = no CYP3A4 inhibition
  • "TAC = DIABETES, TREMOR, PRES"
  • "CSA = HIRSUTISM, GINGIVAL, HYPERLIPID, GOUT, HTN, TMA"
  • "SRL = PROTEINURIA, WOUND HEALING, HYPERLIPID, PNEUMONITIS"
  • "MMF = GI, BONE MARROW, TERATOGENIC"

10. 10. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

Target: ≥37/46 (80%)

11. 11. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Short Question (5 marks): "Tacrolimus target trough ranges and key drug interactions."

  • Kidney: Early (0–3mo) 10–15; Late (>3mo) 5–10 ng/mL
  • Liver: 5–10; Heart/Lung: 10–15
  • Strong inhibitors (↓ dose): Voriconazole (1/10–1/3), Itraconazole/Posaconazole (1/3), Fluconazole (1/2), Clarithromycin/Erythromycin (1/2), Verapamil/Diltiazem (1/2)
  • Strong inducers (AVOID/↑ dose): Rifampicin (AVOID, ↓ 90%), Carbamazepine/Phenytoin/Phenobarbital, St John's Wort
  • Azithromycin safe (no CYP3A4 inhibition)

2. Viva (1 min): "Kidney transplant patient on tacrolimus 4mg BD, started on voriconazole for aspergillosis. Tacrolimus level next day 45 ng/mL (target 5–10). Action?"

  • Voriconazole = potent CYP3A4/P-gp inhibitor → ↑ tacrolimus 5–10x
  • IMMEDIATELY reduce tacrolimus to 1/10–1/3 dose (e.g., 4mg BD → 0.5mg daily or 1mg daily)
  • Monitor tacrolimus level DAILY until stable
  • Re-titrate after voriconazole stopped (return to baseline over 3–5 days)

3. Ward Round (30 sec): "Transplant patient on ciclosporin, prescribed clarithromycin for chest infection. Alternative?"

  • Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 → ↑ ciclosporin 2–3x
  • SWITCH to azithromycin (no CYP3A4 inhibition)
  • If clarithromycin essential: ↓ ciclosporin 50–66%, monitor levels daily

4. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Tac: Kidney 10-15→5-10; Liver 5-10; Heart/Lung 10-15
  • CsA: Kidney 200-300→100-200; C2 800-1500
  • SRL: 4-12; EVR: 3-8
  • Vori + Tac = 1/10–1/3 dose (DAILY levels); Fluconazole = 1/2; Clari = 1/2
  • Rifampicin = AVOID (↓ 90%); Azithromycin = SAFE
  • Tac toxicity = PTDM, Tremor, PRES, Nephro (afferent vasoconstriction)
  • CsA toxicity = Hirsutism, Gingival, TMA, Gout, HTN, Hyperlipid
  • SRL = Proteinuria, Wound healing, Hyperlipid, Pneumonitis
  • MMF = GI, Bone marrow, Teratogenic

12. 12. 📚 Summary Card

IMMUNOSUPPRESSANT TDM:
TAC: Kidney 10-15→5-10; Liver 5-10; Heart/Lung 10-15
CSA: Kidney 200-300→100-200; C2 800-1500
SRL: 4-12; EVR: 3-8
VORI + TAC = 1/10 DOSE (DAILY LEVELS)
FLUCO + TAC = 1/2 DOSE
CLARI + CSA = 1/2 DOSE (USE AZITHRO)
RIFA = AVOID (↓ 90%)
TAC = PTDM, TREMOR, PRES; CSA = HIRSUTISM, TMA, GOUT
SRL = PROTEINURIA, WOUND HEALING, HYPERLIPID
MMF = GI, BONE MARROW, TERATOGENIC

topics.title: Lithium
topics.html:
Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Therapeutic Drug Monitoring → Drug-Specific TDM | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (Bipolar, narrow TI, renal monitoring, toxicity classic)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] State therapeutic and toxic ranges
  • [ ] Apply sampling timing (12h post-dose standard)
  • [ ] Execute renal/thyroid monitoring protocol
  • [ ] Identify toxicity features: neurological, renal, endocrine
  • [ ] Manage toxicity: fluid resuscitation, dialysis indications
  • [ ] Recognise drug interactions (NSAIDs, ACEi, Thiazides, etc.)

2. 2. 📊 Therapeutic Range & Sampling

IndicationTherapeutic Range (mmol/L)Toxic Threshold
Acute Mania0.8–1.2>1.5
Maintenance / Prophylaxis0.6–1.0 (some 0.4–0.8)>1.2
Toxicity—>1.5 = moderate, >2.5 = severe / dialysis

1. Sampling Timing — CRITICAL

SampleTimingRationale
Standard trough12h post-dose (±30 min)Steady-state trough; lithium t½ ~18–24h
If once daily (evening)Morning (12h post)Standard practice
If twice daily12h post-evening dose (pre-morning)Most reliable
RandomNot recommendedUse nomogram if unavoidable
Steady state: 5–7 days (5 half-lives); check level at 5–7 days after initiation or dose change

3. 3. ⚖️ Renal Adjustment & Monitoring

1. Dose Adjustment by eGFR/CrCl

eGFR/CrCl (mL/min)Dose AdjustmentMonitoring
>60StandardLevel q3mo; eGFR q6mo; TSH q6–12mo
45–60↓ 25%Level q3mo; eGFR q6mo; TSH q6–12mo
30–45↓ 50%Level q2–3mo; eGFR q3–6mo; TSH q6–12mo
15–30↓ 75% (or avoid/)Level q1–2mo; eGFR q3mo; TSH q6–12mo
<15Avoid if possible; if essential: specialist only, ↓ >75%Level q1mo; eGFR q1–3mo; TSH q6mo
Maintenance dose typically 400–1200mg/day (lithium carbonate 200–600mg 1–2× daily)

2. Mandatory Monitoring Protocol (NICE / BAP)

TestBaselineFrequency (Stable)Frequency (Unstable/Tox)
Lithium LevelDay 5–7Every 3 monthsWeekly until stable
eGFR / CreatinineBaselineEvery 6 monthsEvery 1–3 months
TSH / Thyroid FunctionBaselineEvery 6–12 monthsEvery 6 months
CalciumBaselineAnnuallyEvery 6 months
Weight / BMIBaselineEvery 6 monthsEvery 3 months
U&EsBaselineEvery 6 monthsEvery 1–3 months

4. 4. ☠️ Lithium Toxicity

1. Acute vs Chronic

FeatureAcute Toxicity (Overdose)Chronic Toxicity (Accumulation)
ContextIntentional / accidental overdoseRenal impairment, dehydration, drug interaction, dose increase
Level CorrelationPoor — symptoms lag behind levelBetter correlation — level reflects tissue concentration
Renal EliminationNormal (initially)Impaired — ↓ clearance
NeurologicalLess prominent earlyProminent (tremor, confusion, ataxia, seizures)
GIProminent (N/V, diarrhoea)Less prominent
OutcomeBetter if treated earlyHigher morbidity/mortality; risk of SILENT neurological damage

2. Clinical Features by Severity

SeverityLevelFeatures
Mild1.5–2.0Fine tremor, N/V, diarrhoea, polyuria, polydipsia, mild cognitive impairment
Moderate2.0–2.5Coarse tremor, hyperreflexia, ataxia, dysarthria, confusion, agitation, muscle weakness, ECG changes
Severe>2.5Seizures, coma, hyperthermia, rigidity, permanent neurotoxicity (SILENT: cerebellar atrophy, cognitive decline), cardiovascular collapse
SILENT = Syndrome of Irreversible Lithium-Effectuated Neurotoxicity — cerebellar dysfunction persists after level normalises

3. ECG Changes

ChangeSignificance
T-wave flattening / inversionCommon, non-specific
QT prolongationRisk of Torsades
Sinus node dysfunction / AV blockSevere toxicity

5. 5. 💊 Management of Toxicity

SeverityAction
Mild (1.5–2.0, asymptomatic/minimal)HOLD lithium; IV 0.9% NaCl 1–2L (hydration); correct K⁺/Mg²⁺; monitor level q12–24h; restart at ↓ dose when <1.0
Moderate (2.0–2.5, symptomatic)HOLD lithium; IV 0.9% NaCl 2–3L (aggressive hydration); Furosemide 20–40mg (only if euvolaemic) to ↑ Li⁺ clearance; correct K⁺/Mg²⁺; consider haemodialysis if neuro symptoms; monitor level q6–12h
Severe (>2.5, seizures/coma/neuro)HAEMODIALYSIS (preferred) or CRRT; HD is most effective (high clearance, short duration); Continue dialysis until level <1.0 and clinical improvement; Rebound common — need repeated HD

1. Dialysis Indications (EXACT Criteria)

IndicationThreshold
Level>2.5 mmol/L (acute) OR >4.0 mmol/L (acute overdose)
NeurologicalSeizures, Coma, Severe confusion, Focal deficits
RenalAKI / Oliguria / eGFR <15
CardiacLife-threatening arrhythmia
Failure of conservativeLevel not ↓ with hydration + diuresis
HD removes ~50% of lithium per 4h session; Rebound ↑ 30–50% post-HD (redistribution from tissues) — often need repeated HD

6. 6. 🔄 Key Drug Interactions (↑ Lithium Levels)

DrugMechanismLevel IncreaseManagement
NSAIDs (incl. COX-2)↓ Renal PG → ↓ GFR + ↑ proximal Li⁺ reabsorption25–100%AVOID; use paracetamol; if essential: ↓ Li⁺ dose 25–50%, monitor weekly
ACE Inhibitors↓ Afferent tone → ↓ GFR; ↓ Na⁺ delivery → ↑ Li⁺ reabsorption20–50%Monitor Li⁺ at 1wk, 2wk, 4wk; ↓ Li⁺ dose if needed
ARBsSimilar to ACEi20–50%Same as ACEi
ThiazidesVolume depletion → ↑ proximal Na⁺/Li⁺ reabsorption25–100%AVOID if possible; if essential: ↓ Li⁺ dose 25–50%, monitor weekly
Loop DiureticsLess effect than thiazides; volume depletion10–25%Monitor Li⁺
Potassium-Sparing (Amiloride, Triamterene)↑ Li⁺ reabsorption in collecting duct10–25%Avoid; Amiloride used for Li⁺-induced NDI (paradoxical)
Tetracyclines (Demeclocycline)?VariableMonitor

7. 7. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

PearlDetails
Therapeutic rangeAcute mania: 0.8–1.2; Maintenance: 0.6–1.0 (some 0.4–0.8)
Sampling12h post-dose (trough); steady state at 5–7 days
MonitoringLevel q3mo, eGFR q6mo, TSH q6–12mo (stable)
Toxicity thresholds>1.5 moderate; >2.5 severe/dialysis
Acute vs ChronicAcute = poor level correlation, GI dominant; Chronic = better correlation, neuro dominant, higher mortality
SILENT syndromeIrreversible cerebellar neurotoxicity post-toxicity
HD indicationsLevel >2.5 + neuro symptoms; >4.0 acute; AKI; seizures; failure conservative
Top interactionsNSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, Thiazides → ↑ Li⁺ 25–100% (monitor weekly)
NDI managementAmiloride 5–10mg BD (paradoxically reduces Li⁺ reabsorption)

8. 8. ❓ Viva Questions (10)

QAnswer
1. Lithium therapeutic range for acute mania vs maintenance?Acute mania: 0.8–1.2 mmol/L; Maintenance: 0.6–1.0 mmol/L
2. When to sample lithium level?12h post-dose (trough); steady state at 5–7 days after initiation/dose change
3. Monitoring protocol in stable patient?Level q3mo, eGFR q6mo, TSH q6–12mo, Ca annually, Weight q6mo
4. Acute vs chronic lithium toxicity — key differences?Acute: GI dominant (N/V), poor level correlation; Chronic: Neuro dominant (tremor, ataxia, confusion), better correlation, higher mortality, SILENT risk
5. SILENT syndrome?Syndrome of Irreversible Lithium-Effectuated Neurotoxicity — persistent cerebellar dysfunction (ataxia, dysarthria) after level normalisation
6. Dialysis indications for lithium toxicity?Level >2.5 + neuro symptoms; >4.0 acute; AKI; seizures; life-threatening arrhythmia; failure of conservative management
7. NSAIDs + lithium — effect and management?↑ Li⁺ 25–100% (↓ PG → ↓ GFR + ↑ reabsorption); AVOID NSAIDs; use paracetamol; if essential: ↓ Li⁺ dose 25–50%, monitor weekly
8. Thiazide + lithium — effect?↑ Li⁺ 25–100% (volume depletion → ↑ proximal reabsorption); avoid if possible; ↓ Li⁺ dose, monitor
9. Lithium-induced NDI — management?Amiloride 5–10mg BD (blocks ENaC in collecting duct); ensure water access; consider switching mood stabiliser
10. Lithium level 2.8 mmol/L, confused, coarse tremor. Management?Moderate-severe chronic toxicity; HOLD lithium; IV saline 2–3L + furosemide; Consider HD (level >2.5 + neuro); correct K⁺/Mg²⁺; monitor q6–12h

9. 9. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Acute toxicity = GI symptoms firstAcute = GI (N/V); Chronic = Neuro (tremor, ataxia, confusion)
Level correlates with severityAcute: poor correlation; Chronic: better correlation
Dialysis removes lithiumHD removes ~50% per session; Rebound 30–50% post-HD → repeated HD often needed
Amiloride for NDIParadoxical — amiloride ↑ Li⁺ excretion by blocking ENaC in collecting duct (used therapeutically)
Lithium + ThiazideBoth cause NDI but for different reasons — thiazide ↑ Li⁺ → toxicity risk

Mnemonics:

  • "LITHIUM RANGE" = Mania 0.8–1.2; Maintenance 0.6–1.0; Toxic >1.5; Dialysis >2.5
  • "SAMPLE 12H" = 12h post-dose trough; steady state 5-7 days
  • "MONITOR q3/q6/q12" = Level q3mo, eGFR q6mo, TSH q12mo
  • "ACUTE = GI; CHRONIC = NEURO" = different presentations
  • "SILENT = IRREVERSIBLE CEREBELLAR" = ataxia persists after level normal
  • "DIALYSIS >2.5 + NEURO / >4.0 ACUTE / AKI / SEIZURES"
  • "NSAID/ACEi/ARB/THIAZIDE = ↑ Li⁺ 25-100%" = monitor weekly, avoid if possible
  • "NDI = AMILOLIDE 5-10mg BD" = blocks ENaC

13. 13. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Short Question (5 marks): "Lithium monitoring protocol and toxicity thresholds."

  • Therapeutic: Mania 0.8–1.2; Maintenance 0.6–1.0; Toxic >1.5; Dialysis >2.5
  • Sampling: 12h post-dose trough; steady state 5–7 days
  • Monitoring: Level q3mo, eGFR q6mo, TSH q6–12mo, Ca annually
  • Toxicity: Acute = GI (N/V), poor level correlation; Chronic = Neuro (tremor, ataxia, confusion), better correlation, SILENT risk
  • Dialysis: >2.5 + neuro; >4.0 acute; AKI; seizures

2. Viva (1 min): "Patient on lithium 800mg, started ibuprofen for back pain. 1 week later: coarse tremor, confusion, Li⁺ 2.8. Management?"

  • NSAID-induced lithium toxicity (NSAIDs ↓ PG → ↓ GFR + ↑ proximal reabsorption → ↑ Li⁺ 25–100%)
  • STOP ibuprofen immediately; switch to paracetamol
  • HOLD lithium; IV saline 2–3L + furosemide
  • Consider HD (level >2.5 + neuro symptoms)
  • Monitor Li⁺ q6–12h; restart at ↓ dose when <1.0

3. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on lithium, polyuria 4L/day, hypernatremia. Diagnosis? Treatment?"

  • Lithium-induced NDI (nephrogenic diabetes insipidus)
  • Amiloride 5–10mg BD (blocks ENaC in collecting duct)
  • Ensure free water access
  • Consider switching mood stabiliser if persistent

4. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Lithium range: Mania 0.8–1.2, Maintenance 0.6–1.0, Toxic >1.5
  • Sample: 12h post-dose; steady state 5–7 days
  • Monitor: Level q3mo, eGFR q6mo, TSH q12mo
  • Acute toxicity = GI, poor correlation; Chronic = Neuro, better correlation, SILENT
  • SILENT = irreversible cerebellar damage
  • HD: >2.5+neuro, >4.0 acute, AKI, seizures
  • NSAIDs/ACEi/ARB/Thiazides = ↑ Li⁺ 25–100% (monitor weekly)
  • NDI = Amiloride 5–10mg BD

14. 14. 📚 Summary Card

LITHIUM TDM:
RANGE: Mania 0.8-1.2, Maintenance 0.6-1.0, Toxic >1.5, Dialysis >2.5
SAMPLE: 12h post-dose trough; SS 5-7d
MONITOR: Level q3mo, eGFR q6mo, TSH q12mo
TOXICITY: Acute=GI, Chronic=Neuro (SILENT=irreversible cerebellar)
DIALYSIS: >2.5+neuro; >4.0 acute; AKI; seizures
INTERACTIONS: NSAIDs/ACEi/ARB/Thiazides = ↑ Li⁺ 25-100% (AVOID)
NDI: Amiloride 5-10mg BD
